EVENT_NARRATIVE: Large mature trees were blown down by the strong wind, causing scattered power outages.  $5,000 in damages occurred to phone lines.
Engineers for United Telephone estimated a 125 mph wind gust (at 400 feet AGL) broke off a 7 inch diameter fiberglass antenna mounted on a tower.
A 6 inch diameter tree was blown down.
Shingles were blown off a roof.
Numerous trees were blown down, some falling on homes and cars.  Several fences were also blown down.
Trees were blown down.
Large tree branches were blown down.
Tree branches were blown down.
A large cottonwood tree was blown down.
Trees were uprooted in Edmore, several blocking Main Street.  A grain dryer mounted on the elevator was ripped off.  There were also reports of shingle and vehicle damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The winter storm over central North Dakota began moving northeast producing strong northwest winds over western North Dakotain its wake. The winter storm warning for the west was upgraded to a blizzard warning due to the strong winds. Winds of 30 to45 mph with gust around 50 mph were producing near zero visibilities in blowing snow. No travel was advised by the North Dakota Highway Patrol over much of western North Dakota.

An area of surface low pressure over the central plains moved northeast into northern Wisconsin, spreading snow across portions of southeast North Dakota.  One band of snow over central North Dakota merged with another band moving north out of northeast South Dakota.  This produced a finger of 6 to 8 inches of snow from eastern Sargent county in North Dakota, through the Fargo-Moorhead area, and into southwest Norman county in Minnesota.  Wind speeds increased after the heaviest snow ended.  The strong wind gusts did lower the visibility to near zero at times.  School and after school activities were cancelled in West Fargo, Fargo, Moorhead, and in Ransom county.  The North Dakota Highway Patrol advised no travel in rural Cass and Richland counties and flights out of Hector Airport were cancelled.

The late December winter storm over central North Dakota had moved northeast into northern Minnestoa prompting a winter storm warning for the north central areas of North Dakota.The precipitation began as ice, sleet, then snow over this area. Snowfall of 7 to 8 inches fell. The combination of ice, sleet and snow created very hazardous road conditions and no travel was advised by the North Dakota Highway Patrol.

An Alberta Clipper system dropped southeast into western North Dakota, then reformed over southeast Minnesota.  This brought a band of 4 to 7 inches of snow to a thin corridor from western Benson county into western Walsh county.  Northwest wind speeds picked up behind the low, gusting from 25 to 35 mph.  Most of the snow fell ahead of the low, in the warm sector.  Due to air temperatures right around freezing, blowing snow never became a big problem.

A strong arctic front moved through the northern plains, and produced strong northwest winds.  The strongest winds occurred across the northern Red River Valley, where a MN DOT RWIS site at St. Vincent, MN, recorded a 56 mph wind gust.  Little snow was associated with the frontal passage, but there was about an inch of fluffy snow on the ground at the time.  The worst visibilities were found in open areas.  With little falling snow and mostly sunny conditions above the blowing snow layer, it was considered a ground blizzard.  Slow improvement occurred in the afternoon, with blizzard conditions actually ending in the northern Red River Valley by 3 pm.  Quite a bit of dirt mixed in with the snow, creating a dirt/blowing snow mix.

A ground blizzard existed over parts of east central North Dakota for several hours in the afternoon of February 11th, 2003reducing visibilities to near zero. The white-out conditions were produced by several factors, the combination of several inches of snow already on the ground and wind gusts to 40 mph,  along with scattered snow showers.  The coldfront responsible for the harsh weather exited the region late in the afternoon bringing a rapid improvement to the weather elements across east central North Dakota.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A women, age unknown, left her home walking to the grocery store. She took refuge in an abandon house with no electricity or heat. She died of hypothermia. No other information was available.

A young woman died of hperthermia approxiately several days before finding her body in a open field near her home. Time of  death is unknown. The young woman was intoxicated when she left her home. Nearby weather reporting stations indicated overnight lows between 10 to 20 below zero prior to finding her body.

A strong upper level disturbance combined with an inverted trough at the surface to produce heavy snow across northeast North Dakota.  The event began as light rain, then changed over to snow as cold air was drawn into the system.  The heavy, wet snow broke some tree branches and resulted in scattered power outages.  Rural schools northeast of Devils Lake closed on the 27th.  An unofficial report of 12 inches of snow was received from Edinburg, and was the highest amount recorded.  Otherwise, 6 to 8 inches of snow were common throughout the warning area.

A fairly compact ridge of high pressure brought clear skies and brisk west/northwest winds to eastern North Dakota.  High temperatures on the 8th were only in the single digits above and below zero.  The west/northwest winds of 15 to 25 mph with a few gusts to 35 mph kept temperatures from plunging too far, yet overnight temperatures did drop to 10 below to 20 below zero (or about 30 degrees below normal).  This dropped wind chill temperatures to 40 below to 50 below zero for a prolonged period the night of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  With only a few inches of snow left on the ground (mostly in sheltered areas), these extreme wind chill temperatures disrupted public events and services.  Some schools cancelled or postponed activities, rather than risk unneeded exposure to these elements.  Numerous water main breaks were also reported, due to the prolonged exposure to the cold.

This dropped wind chill temperatures to 40 below to 50 below zero for a prolonged period the night of the 8th into the morning of the 9th.  With only a few inches of snow left on the ground (mostly in sheltered areas), these extreme wind chill temperatures disrupted public events and services.  Some schools cancelled or postponed activities, rather than risk unneeded exposure to these elements.  Numerous water main breaks were also reported, due to the prolonged exposure to the cold.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Very dry conditions, low humidity and wind gusts to 40 mph triggered a 200 acre wild fire just north of McKenzie in Burleigh county. A second, smaller 4 acre fire started just northwest of Bismarck near Pioneer Park. Both fires were contained by members of the Bismarck Rural Fire Department. No damage or injuries were reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure system developed over the Lee of the Rocky Mountain Region Sunday night the 16th of March. It moved intothe Plains and intensified during the early morning hours on Monday, March 17th. The system brought mainly ice to westernNorth Dakota. The heavy coating of ice closed major roads and brought power outages to approximately 300 families insouthwestern North Dakota. The freezing rain also downed approximately 60 power poles across the region. The systemmoved quickly southeast Monday night and the warning was downgraded to an advisory early Tuesday morning the 18th.

The Missouri River near Williston, and its tributaries, rose above flood stage on March 17th. The flooding was caused bymelting snow and ice jams. The flooding was aggravated by an ice storm and heavy rain across western and centralNorth Dakota on the 17th and 18th. Up to two and a half inches of rain fell in some areas. Numerous homes receivedwater damage and several families at Blacktail Dam, Williams county, had to be evacuated by boat. Numerous roadswere impassable due to high water.

Two young adults, one female, the other male, were hospitalized for severe frostbite to the hands and feet Saturday eveningof March 8th. Temperatures ranged from 5 below to 10 below  zero and wind chills between 25 below to 35 below zero.The first victim was a 16 year old female, who became snowbound in her vehicle. She abandoned her vehicle near McCluskyin central Sheridan county and walked approxiatemately one-half mile to a nearby farmhouse where she was taken to ahospital for treatment. The second victim, a 18 year old male, rolled his vehicle in open country between Edgeley and Juddin western LaMoure county. He abandoned his vehicle and walked approxiatemately 200 yards and sought refuge in anabandoned residence. He was airlifted to a nearby hospital for treatment.

A strong upper level disturbance combined with an inverted trough at the surface to produce heavy snow across northeast North Dakota.  The event began as light rain, then changed over to snow as cold air was drawn into the system.  The heavy, wet snow broke some tree branches and resulted in scattered power outages.  Rural schools northeast of Devils Lake closed on the 27th.  An unofficial report of 12 inches of snow was received from Edinburg, and was the highest amount recorded.  Otherwise, 6 to 8 inches of snow were common throughout the warning area.

A large wildfire erupted along the Burleigh and Emmons county line, along the Missouri river, better know as theMcLean Bottoms. The fire was started by an abandon campfire. The fire was aggravated by afternoon temperaturesaround 90 degrees, relative humidities around 10 percent and gusty southerly winds of 25 to 35 mph. The fire burnedin excess of 5000 acres before being contained on the evening of  April 13th. A firefighter was hospitalized for smokeinhalation. Highway 1804 was closed for several hours due to the fire and smoke and the evacuation of recreationenthusiasts from the boat landing and rifle range were ordered by the by the Incident Commander.

Stutsman county firefighters spent the week of April 9th through the 13th battling two dozen wildfires across the countyburning over 9000 acres. Fires flared up near Cleveland, Medina and Windsor in Stutsman county.

A second in a series of low pressure systems moved out of Montana and brought an area of heavy snow across northern third of North Dakota overnight of the 1st of April and lasted through the day on the 2nd. Snowfall amounts ranged between 5 to7 inches across the northern areas of the region. Gusty winds to 30 mph produced low visibilities and dangerous drivingconditions. The system moved southeast Tuesday night to be followed by another winter storm system in the west.

A very tight deformation band of 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across northwest Pembina, southwest Cavalier, and northeast Ramsey counties.  An inverted surface trough extended into the Red River Valley and an upper level disturbance (vorticity maximum) helped to enhance the lift with this system.  The event started as rain, then changed over to heavy, wet snow.

A third in a series of low pressure systems over Wyoming and western South Dakota moved east...southeast and intensifiedover the Northern Plains during the day reaching southern Minnesota and northern Iowa on Thursday,  April 3rd.A stationary surface frontal boundary extended from northeastern Montana through central North Dakota into the area of low pressure.  This promoted overrunning snow across North Dakota with areas of heavy snow across northern NorthDakota. The snowfall combined with easterly winds to 25 mph created areas of blowing and drifting snow and dangerousdriving conditions. Snowfall across northern North Dakota ranged 6 to 10 inches with isolated reports of 14.5 inches.Numerous accidents were contributed icy and snow covered roads resulting in 6 serious injuries and 3 deaths acrossnorthern North Dakota.

Nearly stationary low pressure systems set up from eastern Montana to southern South Dakota, while a tight thermal gradient lined up over North Dakota and Minnesota.  Periodic upper level disturbances rode over this low level feature and helped set the stage for an early April winter storm.  8 inches of snow fell over southeast Towner and northwest Ramsey counties, while 4 to 6 inches fell from this area into Grand Forks county.  A dry northeast flow from high pressure to the north kept this area from expanding in coverage.  Northeast winds gusted from 25 to 35 mph.  With the snow somewhat wet, blowing snow did not become much of a problem.

An area of low pressure over northern Minnesota brought strong north to northwest winds to eastern North Dakota in its wake.  A tree blew over on a parked van in Walhalla.  The owner was getting into the van to go to work at the time and was knocked down.  She suffered minor injuries.  Another tree fell on a house, causing minor structural damage.

A strong thermal gradient remained over the northern plains, while surface low pressure areas stayed over southeast Montana and eastern Nebraska.  As was the case with the April 1-2 winter storm, a fairly strong surface high was anchored just west of Hudson Bay.  An upper level trough over the Rocky Mountains fed several upper level disturbances over the area in the upper southwest jetstream.  4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area along with northeast 25 to 35 mph wind gusts.  Slippery roads were reported along with a few school closures.  The winter storm warning was issued first (at 11:50 am CST) for the Devils Lake region, including Towner, Cavalier, Benson, Ramsey, and Eddy counties.  The winter storm warning was issued later (at 3:30 pm CST) for the other counties.

Snowfall amounts ranged between 5 to7 inches across the northern areas of the region. Gusty winds to 30 mph produced low visibilities and dangerous drivingconditions. The system moved southeast Tuesday night to be followed by another winter storm system in the west.

A third in a series of low pressure systems over Wyoming and western South Dakota moved east...southeast and intensifiedover the Northern Plains during the day reaching southern Minnesota and northern Iowa on Thursday,  April 3rd.A stationary surface frontal boundary extended from northeastern Montana through central North Dakota into the area of low pressure.  This promoted overrunning snow across North Dakota with areas of heavy snow across northern NorthDakota. The snowfall combined with easterly winds to 25 mph created areas of blowing and drifting snow and dangerousdriving conditions. Snowfall across northern North Dakota ranged 6 to 10 inches with isolated reports of 14.5 inches.Numerous accidents were contributed icy and snow covered roads resulting in 6 serious injuries and 3 deaths acrossnorthern North Dakota.

A strong thermal gradient remained over the northern plains, while surface low pressure areas stayed over southeast Montana and eastern Nebraska.  As was the case with the April 1-2 winter storm, a fairly strong surface high was anchored just west of Hudson Bay.  An upper level trough over the Rocky Mountains fed several upper level disturbances over the area in the upper southwest jetstream.  4 to 8 inches of snow fell across the area along with northeast 25 to 35 mph wind gusts.  Slippery roads were reported along with a few school closures.  The winter storm warning was issued first (at 11:50 am CST) for the Devils Lake region, including Towner, Cavalier, Benson, Ramsey, and Eddy counties.  The winter storm warning was issued later (at 3:30 pm CST) for the other counties.

A very tight deformation band of 6 to 8 inches of snow fell across northwest Pembina, southwest Cavalier, and northeast Ramsey counties.  An inverted surface trough extended into the Red River Valley and an upper level disturbance (vorticity maximum) helped to enhance the lift with this system.  The event started as rain, then changed over to heavy, wet snow.

A large wildfire erupted along the Burleigh and Emmons county line, along the Missouri river, better know as theMcLean Bottoms. The fire was started by an abandon campfire. The fire was aggravated by afternoon temperaturesaround 90 degrees, relative humidities around 10 percent and gusty southerly winds of 25 to 35 mph. The fire burnedin excess of 5000 acres before being contained on the evening of  April 13th. A firefighter was hospitalized for smokeinhalation. Highway 1804 was closed for several hours due to the fire and smoke and the evacuation of recreationenthusiasts from the boat landing and rifle range were ordered by the by the Incident Commander.

Nearly stationary low pressure systems set up from eastern Montana to southern South Dakota, while a tight thermal gradient lined up over North Dakota and Minnesota.  Periodic upper level disturbances rode over this low level feature and helped set the stage for an early April winter storm.  8 inches of snow fell over southeast Towner and northwest Ramsey counties, while 4 to 6 inches fell from this area into Grand Forks county.  A dry northeast flow from high pressure to the north kept this area from expanding in coverage.  Northeast winds gusted from 25 to 35 mph.  With the snow somewhat wet, blowing snow did not become much of a problem.

Accompanied by 50 mph winds.

Heavy rain and strong winds accompanied the storm.
Hail covered the ground.
Farmer reported 20% of his canola crop destroyed.
Mainly pea size hail along with very heavy rain.
Weak funnel briefly touched down in rural farm field.
A brief touchdown in a land fill then lifted and disipated. No damage reported.
Reported by a NWS cooperative observer.
An 8 inch diameter tree limb was blown down.
Tree limbs were blown down at the Turtle River State Park.

Large branches were blown down.

The tornado had multiple brief touchdowns along the path, but had a dust and debris cloud.  Witnesses also heard a roaring sound.  Crops were damaged along the path.
6 inch diameter tree branches were blown down.
Six inch diameter tree branches were blown down.
The Devils Lake AWOS measured a 62 mph wind gust.
The tornado tracked through open country.
A brief tornado touchdown was reported.
Street flooding was reported.
Water was reported over a road.
Trees and large tree limbs were blown down causing some power outages.
The hail was accompanied by strong winds.
A 66 mph wind gust was measured by a RAWS mesonet site at the Tewaukon National Wildlife Refuge.
Tree branches were blown down.
A weigh station near Mooreton measured the wind gust along Interstate 29.
A tower was blown over.
Wind driven hail damaged shingles and shattered 15 windows.
12 to 15 inch diameter trees were uprooted by the strong wind.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Several rounds of thunderstorms brought 5 to 8 inches of rainfall to portions of Richland county.  The NWS cooperative observing station at Lidgerwood reported 6.01 inches of rain from 6/22 to 6/28.  The Wild Rice River at Abercrombie rose to around 12.22 feet on 6/28, just over its moderate flood stage of 12 feet.  Two locations along Interstate 29 experienced prolonged flooding, one at mile marker 13 near Great Bend and another at mile marker 29 near Mooreton.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Four inch tree limbs snapped in the town of North Lemmon.
The storm wiped out 1,175 acres of crop from various sizes of hail ranging from pea to golfball.
Large tree branches were blown down.
Large tree limbs were blown down.
The tornado touched down in an open field north of Highway 5.
A 64 mph wind gust was measured by a RAWS mesonet site in the Sheyenne National Grasslands.
Lightning struck a telephone pole and knocked the transformer to the ground.  Cable and telephone service were knocked out in town.
55 mph winds were also reported at the time.
Three trees were blown down and one window was broken by the strong wind.

This tornado originated near Altona, Manitoba, Canada.  The tornado tracked across the international border northeast of Neche, and continued southeast for about a mile across a wheat field at F1 intensity.  The tornado skipped along for another 4.5 miles at F0 intensity.
The wind driven hail destroyed crops.
A large tree was uprooted.
The tornado touched down about 1 mile north of Highway 5.
8 inch diameter tree limbs were broken.
Street flooding was reported along U.S. Highway 2.
Several large elm trees were blown over.
Power outages were reported by law enforcement.
The strong wind caused a barn to collapse.
Briefly touched down in open field.
Tornado touched down in open country just north of the South Dakota border. Tornado lifted before crossing into South Dakota.
Several funnel clouds were sighted, one over the city of Elgin and two more 6 and 12 miles north of Elgin.
A brief touchdown was reported in an open field.
The hail fell on the north side of the city.

Slow moving thunderstorms, mostly on the south side of Fargo, dropped 2 to 4 inches of rain in 10 minutes.  Bumper deep water was reported near the 1400 block south of 25th Street, Arrowhead Road, and the University Drive underpass of Interstate 94.
The tornado briefly touched down in an open field.

Eighteen 80 foot tall 345 kV high voltage power transmission towers were blown down in several sections to the Cass county line.  A section of Highway 32 north of Oriska was briefly closed where the wires fell on the road.
A 112 mph wind gust was measured at a Minnkota Power Company wind turbine site.
Large trees were blown down and power outages were reported.
Power lines were blown down over the highway.
Many trees were blown down.
7 large grain bins were damaged at the Evergreen Farmers Cooperative.
Numerous trees were blown down.
A metal quonset and a garage were destroyed by the wind.  Pieces of debris were flung into the surrounding fields.
A 30x60 foot pole barn roof was blown off, while the structure was totalled.
Seven 80 foot tall 345 KV high voltage power transmission towers were blown down.
Numerous large trees were snapped off at the base.

Numerous large trees were snapped off at the base.  Power went out in town causing some residents to have sewer backup.  Cass County Electric Cooperative reported 40 power poles downed between Tower City and Gardner with 1300 customers without power.
Power lines were blown down.
100 large trees were uprooted or snapped off in town.  Trees fell on vehicles and homes.
Three 80,000 bushel steel grain bins were caved in at the Gardner elevator complex.  A small wooden barn was destroyed.  Large trees were also blown down all over town.
A 40x80 foot barn roof collapsed and other farm buildings were damaged.
Four tractor-trailer rigs were blown off Interstate 29 between mile posts 81 and 84.  Traffic had to be rerouted for 1.5 hours while the vehicles were removed.
Two tractor-trailer rigs were blown Interstate 29 between mile posts 93 and 94.
The Grand Forks ASOS measured the wind gust.
The wind gust was measured at the NWS site on the western edge of Grand Forks.
Street flooding was reported in south Fargo.

Pea to golfball size hail accumulated up to a foot in places stripping paint off several buildings. Rainfall with the storm totaled 2.5 inches. The hail also caused 50 to 100 percent loss to 47,000 acres of cropland.
Several trees 6-8 inches in diameter snapped and 12 foot evergreens bent over. Reported by a NWS cooperative observer.
Public reported could not  keep vehicle on the road.
Tornado dipped up and down remaining over open country.
Hail covered the ground.
Numerous funnel clouds were reported with the storm.
Tornado touched down in open country and moved east northeast remaining over open country then dissipating 4 miles south southwest of Mercer.
Reported by a NWS cooperative observer.
Tornado briefly touched down in open country.
Large 30,000 bushel steel grain bins at the Peak elevator complex were crushed by the wind.  Numerous large trees were also blown down and augers were twisted and broken.
A tornado touched down near Lake Alice National Wildlife Refuge.
A tornado briefly touched down.
A Grand Forks, ND, couple, travelling in the area, videotaped this touchdown and the 1735 CST and 1754 CST touchdowns.
Numerous large branches and trees were blown down.  Many power lines were also blown down with scattered power outages.
Wind driven hail caused partial crop damage over about 20 sections of farmland.
Water flowed over several low water crossings in southeast Richland county.  In Hankinson, Second street southeast was closed.
A brief touchdown was reported.
A brief touchdown was reported near the county line.
A tornado was reported.
A farmer watched a tornado in a field, which then moved into a slough and lifted water 100 feet in the air.
A brief touchdown occurred near Lake Alice National Wildlife Refuge.

A tornado travelled from just west of Pembina in Pembina county, to the Red River about 2 miles south southeast of Pembina, then continued on for about one-half of a mile in Kittson county southeast of St. Vincent.
A large tree was blown down.
Large tree branches were broken off and smaller trees severely bent by the strong wind.
A NDAWN mesonet wind sensor reported a 63 mph wind gust.
Tree branches were blown down.
A large tree was snapped off.
Trees were blown down in Mayville.

A woman leaving work was struck by lightning as she walked to her car.  She suffered no visible injuries, but was taken to the emergency room for observation.  She later reported seizure-like muscle twitches.
Mostly pea size hail with this storm.
Two grain bins, 25 feet tall,  received damage.

The wind destroyed five (5) grain bins with the debris landing on several vehicles which sustained damage. Ten inch diameter trees snapped. The storm was accompanied by one and three-quarter inch hail (1.75).
Several thousand acres of cropland damaged or destroyed.
Reported by a NWS cooperative observer.
A few trees down.
Numerous trees and power lines down.
One inch hail also accompanied the storm.
The hail occurred at Strawberry Lake recreational area.
Heavy rain reported with the storm.

Tornado touched down in open country and traveled north...northeast reaching the municipal airport causing damage to the airport roof and extensive damage to three 44,000 thousand bushel bins and minor damage to other bins. Hail up to one and three-quarter inch (1.75) caused extensive damage to crops.

Tornado briefly touched down in a farmstead destorying a large pole shed and damaging several pieces of large farm equipment. Debris from the shed was scattered a quarter mile away. Several grain bins were twisted on their foundations.

The storm was accompanied by two inch diameter hail (2.00). Numerous north facing windows broken out of homes and buildings. Numerous trees and power lines downed by the wind. A six (6) mile wide swath of crops from Adrian to near Verona received nearly 100 percent loss.
Tornado touched down in open country snapping four (4) foot diameter trees.
Hail damaged numerous vehicles in and around the Medora area.

Tornado touched down just northwest of Crosby ripping a 40 foot wide streak through a forest area where no trees were left standing. Trees on either side were still standing. The tornado skipped through Crosby tearing portions of roofs off a couple of buildings. Large trees were either damaged or toppled. A 1,800 pound bail of hay was lifted over a 4 foot fence and dropped on the other side. Additional minor damage was reported in and around the city before the tornado lifted just southeast of town.
Reported by a NWS cooperative observer.
Large branches were blown down.

Three of four 44,000 bushel grain bins were blown over at the Crete Elevator complex.  The fourth, which was full of grain, remained standing.  Eight 40,000 bushel grains bins were also damaged and one 40,000 bushel grain bin was blown down.
Wind driven hail damaged vinyl siding and some shingles.  Windows were also broken out.
Large trees were snapped.
Three 5,000 bushel steel grain bins were torn off their anchor bolts and destroyed.
Large tree limbs were broken off.  A television antenna and a satellite dish were also torn off a roof.
Windows were broken out by the wind driven hail.
Power outages were reported in rural areas west of Cogswell.
The AWOS at the Gwinner Airport reported a 63 mph wind gust.
A Davis wind sensor reported an 81 mph wind gust.

Two 30,000 bushel grain bins were destroyed by the strong wind.  The tops of two 4,000 bushel grain silos were damaged.  A flag pole and large trees were blown over.  Ten homes had broken windows, damaged siding, and damaged roofing.
A single-wide 35 foot mobile home was rolled off its foundation and destroyed.  An empty fuel oil tank was blown into its side.
Two steel 3,500 bushel hopper bins were blown over and numerous large trees were uprooted.  The city park reported about $3,000 in damages.  Scattered power outages also occurred.
Large trees were blown down.
A 35 foot tall pine tree was blown down along with numerous branches.
A large tree was blown across a driveway.
The hail knocked out some windows.

Tornado touched down over open country and traveled east northeast causing damage to the recreational area of Brush Lake. Several roofs were blown off lake cabins with one cabin being lifted off its foundation. A two by four (2x4) piece of wood impacted a parked semi-trailor truck and two 1,000 pound bales of hay were destroyed. The tornado crossed the county line into Sheridan county and moved southeast.

The tornado crossed the McLean/Sheridan county line and moved east southeast impacting the town of Pickardville. Numerous buildings and grain elevators were damaged in the small community. One young boy was injured after being thrown to the ground and hit by flying debris.
Damage confined to a broken windshield.
Two 4,000 bushel steel grain bins were ripped off their anchor bolts.  A roof was also torn off a 35x70 foot steel shed and the debris was carried one-half mile into the adjacent fields.
One 5,000 bushel steel grain bin was torn off its anchor bolts and portions of it were scattered for 200 yards into an adjacent field.
6 inch diameter tree branches were blown down.
Scattered power outages were reported.
Several 12-18 inch trees were broken off at their bases.
Large tree branches and several wooden fence sections were blown down.
One 4,000 bushel steel grain bin was ripped off its anchor bolts and flattened in a field.

Four 75 foot tall 30,000 bushel grain bins were crushed by the strong wind.  These bins were about half full at the time.  A roof was also blown off a fertilizer plant and numerous trees were uprooted.  Several trees fell on rooftops and power was out in town for several hours.
Hail up to three-quarter inch (.75) in diameter accompanied the storm.
Grain bin sustained major damage eight miles west of Dickinson.
Numerous large trees down. Reported by a NWS cooperative observer.
Large tree limbs broken.
Several trees and power lines down due to high winds.
Numerous broken windshields reported. Estimated 60 mph winds damaged trees and several grain bins.
Winds estimated at 55 mph flipped a couple campers over on their sides and a picnic shelter was blown over at Blacktail Dam Recreational area.
Tree branches up to 4 inches in diameter broken.
Power outage for several hours reported. Heavy rain accompanied the storm.

Wind damage to nearly all farmsteads between Napoleon and Fredonia with one family home destroyed. Power outage occurred for several hours in the city of Napoleon along with numerous trees uprooted and one home receiving major roof damage. On the outskirts of Napoleon 30-4 thousand pound each thresher machines tipped on their sides. A 75,000 and two 25,000 bushel grain bins destroyed along with a well/drilling and repair shop destroyed in the city of Fredonia.
The hail was accompanied by 60 mph (52 knots) wind gust breaking out a window in a home.
Several tree limbs down.
Wind gust to 52 knots(60 mph) accompanied the storm.
